output.answer:
## High-Priority Issues the City Should Be Watching

Here is a cross-document synthesis of the issues that appear most urgent or consequential based on patterns across the memos. These are what the memos *say*, not independently verified facts.

---

### 1. Public Safety Dispatch System — Unresolved Blocker Defects Before Go-Live
**[MAR-009]**
The memos suggest this is the single highest-stakes technical risk the city faces right now. Of 11 remaining open tickets on the CAD/RMS replacement system, 6 are classified as blocker defects — meaning they could prevent go-live. Only 4 of those 6 have patches pending or installed. The system covers law enforcement, fire, and EMS dispatch for the entire region. A failure or delay here would have cascading public safety consequences. The go-live target is late summer/early fall 2026, and training hasn't started yet (curriculum due April 2026). Council should be watching whether those remaining 2 unpatched blockers get resolved on schedule.

---

### 2. Homelessness — Rising Service Requests and Declining Outreach Effectiveness
**[APR-015], [APR-003]**
Comparing February and March 2026 Clean and Safe reports reveals a troubling trend: outreach interactions *dropped* from 55 to 41, housing arrangements dropped from 11 to 4, while arrests *increased* from 80 to 96, citations jumped from 91 to 176, and total service requests rose 9% (351 in March vs. 323 in February). Occupied Vehicle Nuisance reports specifically surged 35%. The memos suggest enforcement is intensifying while housing placements are declining — a pattern worth scrutiny. The collaboration with UNR Police also signals the problem is spreading geographically **[APR-015]**.

---

### 3. Parking Enforcement — Two Unresolved Systemic Gaps
**[APR-005]**
The memos identify two enforcement mechanisms that remain broken: California registered owner mail notifications are stalled pending a vendor quote review, and Nevada DMV holds are blocked pending state-level action from the Nevada Department of Public Safety — neither of which the city controls. These gaps mean a significant share of violators (particularly out-of-state drivers) face no meaningful consequence. The city has collected $633,339 since June 2025, but adjudication is reducing balances by 55–60%, and the revenue picture depends on closing these enforcement gaps.

---

### 4. Senior Housing Safety — Ongoing Gaps at Vintage at the Crossings
**[APR-008]**
The memos suggest a vulnerable population (seniors at 11565 Old Virginia Road) is still in an incomplete safety situation. As of April 2026, a detailed evacuation plan has not yet been finalized, ADA door access has not yet been installed, and a full building unit inspection is still planned but not completed. Two fire inspections were conducted in February, but the follow-up actions remain open. Given the population involved, delays in the evacuation plan and building inspection are worth monitoring closely.

---

### 5. High Volume of Public Records Requests — Possible Legal or Investigative Activity
**[APR-009]**
The memos note an unusually high concentration of records requests in a single week (April 10–16), with at least 58 requests visible in a truncated report, and individual requestors submitting 7–9 requests on a single day. The memo itself flags this as suggesting "institutional or legal inquiry activity." While the memo doesn't identify what is being investigated, the pattern — dominated by police reports, fire records, and building permits — warrants awareness that the city may be subject to active legal or journalistic scrutiny.

---

### 6. Code Enforcement Pressure in Ward 1
**[JAN-009], [APR-003], [FEB-007]**
Multiple memos converge on Ward 1 as a stress point. The Love Your Block program selected Ward 1 specifically because of its high concentration of residential code violations **[JAN-009]**. City Walk operations in December 2025 generated 18 code enforcement cases in a single month **[FEB-007]**, and the Clean and Safe Team continues generating dozens of code cases monthly **[APR-003]**. The memos suggest demand for code enforcement is outpacing capacity, and the Love Your Block program is a partial, grant-funded response — not a structural fix.

---

### Summary Table

| Issue | Key Memo(s) | Nature of Risk |
|---|---|---|
| CAD/RMS blocker defects | MAR-009 | Operational/public safety |
| Homelessness trend (more enforcement, fewer placements) | APR-015, APR-003 | Policy/humanitarian |
| Parking enforcement gaps (CA, NV DMV) | APR-005 | Revenue/legal |
| Senior housing safety gaps | APR-008 | Vulnerable population |
| Unusual public records request volume | APR-009 | Legal/reputational |
| Ward 1 code enforcement pressure | JAN-009, APR-003, FEB-007 | Neighborhood stability |

MAR-009.title: Monthly Hexagon Update - March 2026
MAR-009.tldr: The City of Reno's Public Safety Dispatch department provides the March 2026 monthly status update on the Regional Hexagon CAD/RMS Modernization Project, which is replacing aging dispatch and records systems for law enforcement, fire, EMS, and regional partners. Of the original 73 priority tickets, only 11 remain open (6 of which are blocker defects), and functional acceptance testing is actively underway. The project remains on schedule for a late summer/early fall go-live, with mandatory user training, 24/7 transition support, and final interface validation planned ahead of cutover.
MAR-009.key_stats: 73 initial priority tickets; 11 currently open; 6 of 11 open tickets are blocker defects; 4 of 6 blockers have patches pending or installed; go-live target is late summer/early fall 2026.
MAR-009.key_decisions: No formal Council decisions requested in this memo; the update is provided for ongoing oversight and transparency. Minor timeline adjustments for testing alignment were made with no impact to the go-live date.
MAR-009.action_items: Upcoming deliverables include: completion of remaining CAD/RMS testing cycles; release of first dispatcher and field user training curriculum (April 2026); integrated end-to-end and performance load testing; finalization of data migration and freeze date; and preparation of the final cutover/go-live readiness package and regional communications materials.

APR-005.title: Parking Program Updates – February 2026
APR-005.tldr: The City of Reno's Parking Program update for February 2026 reports $633,339.85 in total parking fines and fees collected since DMV registered owner data access was reinstated on June 20, 2025. Adjudication activity in January and February 2026 reduced original violation balances by roughly 55–60%. Software vendor AIMS is pursuing California mail notifications and awaiting state action on Nevada DMV holds. IPS technicians are ongoing in replacing solar panels and batteries on single-space meters, with all critical issues expected resolved by end of March 2026.
APR-005.key_stats: Total collected since June 20, 2025: $633,339.85 across 18,156 tickets paid. February 2026: 1,206 tickets paid, $54,058.75 collected. January 2026 adjudications: 89 cases, original balance $8,914.00 reduced to $3,545.00. February 2026 adjudications: 119 cases, original balance $11,441.00 reduced to $5,813.00.
APR-005.key_decisions: Staff will continue monitoring revenue collections weekly and may send unpaid citations to collections. Staff will review AIMS quote for California mail notifications before proceeding. Nevada DMV holds remain pending state-level action from the Nevada Department of Public Safety.
APR-005.action_items: 1. Monitor parking revenue collections weekly. 2. Review AIMS quote for California registered owner mail notifications and determine next steps. 3. Await Nevada Department of Public Safety action to complete DMV holds setup. 4. Work with AIMS to develop expanded reports with additional metrics for future memos. 5. Ensure IPS resolves all battery-critical meter issues by end of March 2026.

FEB-005.title: Washoe County School District Intern Program
FEB-005.tldr: The Community Engagement and Services Department launched a pilot high school internship program in fall 2025 in partnership with the Washoe County School District's Career Technical Education program. Four students contributed 240 hours across four City departments in the fall; eight students are contributing 480 hours across six departments in spring 2026. The program provides hands-on local government experience and academic credit at no direct cost to the City, though it requires an administrative investment. Staff will evaluate the program's long-term sustainability at the end of the academic year.
FEB-005.key_stats: Fall 2025: 4 students, 4 departments, ~240 total internship hours. Spring 2026: 8 students, 6 departments, 480 total internship hours projected.
FEB-005.key_decisions: No formal Council decision requested; memo is informational. Staff will evaluate program sustainability and possible continuity at the conclusion of the current academic year.
FEB-005.action_items: Community Engagement and Services Department staff to evaluate the pilot program—including available staff capacity—at the end of the 2025-2026 academic year to determine long-term sustainability and possible program continuity.

JAN-009.title: Love Your Block Program Year Two Updates
JAN-009.tldr: The City of Reno's Love Your Block program is entering its second year, funded by Bloomberg's Center for Public Innovation, with Ward 1 selected as the focus area due to high concentrations of residential code violations. The 2026 program features two grant rounds: the CommUNITY Grant for neighborhood beautification and community events, and a second round for individual home exterior improvements. Applications for the CommUNITY Grant open February 13 and close March 31, 2026, with awards announced April 22. The second round opens May 1, 2026, on a rolling basis until funds are exhausted.
JAN-009.key_stats: Two grant funding rounds in 2026; Ward 1 selected as focus area based on high concentration of residential code violations; CommUNITY Grant open February 13–March 31, 2026; awards announced April 22, 2026; implementation phase runs late April through end of September; second round opens May 1, 2026 on a rolling basis.
JAN-009.key_decisions: Ward 1 selected as the 2026 program focus area in coordination with Code Enforcement; two distinct grant categories established to support both larger neighborhood initiatives and individual home improvements; rolling application basis for second grant round until funding is exhausted.
JAN-009.action_items: Open CommUNITY Grant applications on February 13, 2026; close CommUNITY Grant applications on March 31, 2026; subcommittee to review and score submissions in April 2026; announce grant awards on April 22, 2026; begin project implementation late April through end of September 2026; open second application round on May 1, 2026 on a rolling basis.

JAN-013.title: 2026 Reno Police Department Beat-Based Town Halls and Neighborhood Advisory Board Presentations
JAN-013.tldr: The Reno Police Department will host five beat-based town halls across the city in 2026, organized by North, Central, and South police beats, to share public safety information and gather community input. The inaugural event is scheduled for February 12, 2026, at North Valleys High School, with doors opening at 5:00 p.m. and the program beginning at 6:00 p.m. RPD will also conduct annual presentations at all Neighborhood Advisory Boards focused on ward-specific crime statistics and community policing initiatives. A media availability with Chief Nance and Mayor Schieve is set for January 29 to launch the series.
JAN-013.key_stats: Five town halls planned: two in the North Beat (Wards 1, 4, 5), one in the Central Beat (Wards 1, 2, 3), two in the South Beat (Wards 2, 3, 6); inaugural venue (North Valleys High School) accommodates up to 500 attendees and supports live streaming.
JAN-013.key_decisions: Expansion of RPD community engagement through beat-based town halls and annual NAB presentations; adoption of a replicable event model including resource booths, Rolling Recreation Center activities at no cost, and food trucks for each town hall.
JAN-013.action_items: Finalize detailed agenda for February 12 town hall; collect community questions via askrpd@reno.gov ahead of first event; issue press release and conduct media availability on January 29 (1:00–1:30 p.m.) at the Public Safety Center; finalize and publish specific venues and dates for remaining four town halls; send Council invitations and advance NAB presentation schedule once calendar is confirmed.

APR-009.title: 04.21.26 Weekly Reports Public Records Request, Media Requests, and Code Enforcement Services Requests
APR-009.tldr: This memorandum transmits weekly activity reports from the City Clerk's Office, Strategic Communications, and Code Enforcement to the Mayor and City Council covering April 10–16, 2026. The attached reports detail public records requests received by category (police, fire, and general), media inquiries handled by Strategic Communications, and code enforcement service requests. The public records log shows a high volume of requests, predominantly for police reports, fire department records, and building/permit documents. Multiple requestors submitted numerous records requests on the same date, suggesting institutional or legal inquiry activity.
APR-009.key_stats: Reporting period: April 10–16, 2026. Public records log run date: April 17, 2026 at 2:02 AM. At least 58 public records requests are visible across the truncated 14-page report. Dominant request types: Police Reports, Building Plans/Blueprints/Permits, Fire Prevention Records, and Incident Reports. Notable high-volume individual requestors include Yen Dinh (7+ requests on 4/10) and Wendy Digiorno (9+ requests on 4/11).
APR-009.action_items: Mayor and City Council to review attached reports: (A) Public Records Requests – City Clerk's Office, (B) Weekly Media Requests – Strategic Communications, (C) Weekly Code Enforcement Service Requests – Code Enforcement.

APR-003.title: Clean and Safe Team Homeless Actions and Outcomes - February 2026
APR-003.tldr: The Clean and Safe Team's February 2026 report details interdepartmental efforts to address homelessness in Reno, including 55 outreach interactions resulting in 11 housing arrangements and 2 shelter arrangements. Enforcement activities included 91 citations, 80 arrests, 23 encampment clean-ups, and 127 yards of trash collected. Reno Direct received 323 service requests related to homeless concerns, occupied vehicles, trespassing, and camping, a 1% decrease from January. Key program updates include 7 individuals facilitated into the Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program and ongoing deployment of Mobile Camera Trailers at chronic hotspot locations.
APR-003.key_stats: 55 outreach interactions; 11 housing arrangements; 2 shelter arrangements; 91 citations; 80 arrests (17 felony, 63 misdemeanor); 2 large and 21 smaller encampment clean-ups; 127 yards of trash collected; 42 occupied vehicles posted; 10 vehicles towed; 1 park exclusion; 43 Code Enforcement cases; 104 Allied Universal Security site visits; 761 Park and River Ranger site visits; 71 Ranger interactions with unsheltered individuals; 323 Reno Direct service requests; 7 individuals facilitated into Motel Assistance Program; 4 individuals transitioned from Motel Assistance to permanent housing
APR-003.key_decisions: Established a response process under the City of Reno and NDOT Interlocal Agreement (ILA) to address encampments on NDOT Right-of-Ways; Washoe County Guardian's Office agreed to take over motel payments for two clients who reached the limit of the Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program; City of Reno and Cares Campus Collaboration meetings (launched Fall 2025) continued to streamline transitions of shelter clients with income into permanent housing
APR-003.action_items: Continue Mobile Camera Trailer Pilot Program at Spokane Street and Galletti Way locations; deliver Verification of Homelessness letter to individual at Wells Avenue & Kuenzli Street; submit Motel Assistance paperwork for client met at CARES Campus; identify and submit alternative housing applications for couple evicted from Cheryl Apartments and moved to EconoLodge; follow up on housing plan for client whose living arrangement with mother was ruled out at Competency Court hearing

MAR-010.title: Park Partners Pilot Program at Oxbow and Summit Ridge Park
MAR-010.tldr: The Parks and Recreation Department is launching the Park Partners pilot program at Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park in April 2026. The program creates a structured way for community members to report safety concerns and maintenance issues via signage, phone numbers, and a QR code linked to Reno Direct. Active reporters may be invited to volunteer for hands-on park upkeep tasks. The pilot runs through December 2026, with quarterly updates to City Council and potential expansion to other parks if successful.
MAR-010.key_stats: Pilot runs April–December 2026 at 2 parks (Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park); quarterly updates to City Council; potential future expansion to Teglia's Paradise Park and Virginia Lake Park.
MAR-010.key_decisions: Approval/awareness of the Park Partners pilot program launch at Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park; program evaluation criteria include quantity of service requests reported and number of dedicated volunteers.
MAR-010.action_items: Install signs at Oxbow Nature Study and Summit Ridge Park in March 2026; hold media event with Communications Department in early April 2026; track reports via Reno Direct; provide quarterly updates to City Council; evaluate program in December 2026; expand to additional parks if successful.

APR-008.title: Vintage at the Crossings Community Meeting Update
APR-008.tldr: This memo updates the Mayor and Council on follow-up actions taken after a February 10, 2026, community meeting at Vintage at the Crossings Apartments, a senior housing community at 11565 Old Virginia Road. City departments including Community Engagement and Services, Code Enforcement, and Reno Fire Department have conducted outreach, delivered updated materials, and presented fire safety information to residents. Property management (Vintage at the Crossings and Asset Living) has completed multiple maintenance work orders and reinstated or improved several resident services. Additional enhancements including ADA door access, parking lot speed bumps, and expanded health programming are planned or underway.
APR-008.key_stats: Approximately 30 residents attended the March 26, 2026, Reno Fire Department fire safety presentation. Overnight security presence extended to cover 9:00 p.m. to 5:00 a.m., seven days per week. Two fire inspections completed by Summit Fire and Security (annual on February 9; five-year on February 26).
APR-008.key_decisions: No formal council decisions requested; memo is informational. City staff will continue collaborating with Vintage at the Crossings and Asset Living management to address resident concerns and coordinate City department assistance as needed.
APR-008.action_items: Reno Fire Department Fire Prevention is developing a more detailed evacuation plan for the property. Community Engagement and Services staff will continue working with Vintage at the Crossings and Asset Living on outstanding and emerging resident concerns. Planned property improvements include electronic ADA access installation, pet relief area improvements, smoking area upgrades, full building unit inspection, parking lot speed bumps, expanded snow removal coordination, and outside vendor health/wellness programming.

APR-010.title: Reno Direct & Service Requests Report – March 2026
APR-010.tldr: The March 2026 Reno Direct report summarizes constituent interactions and service request activity across all city departments. The centralized customer service center handled 2,819 total interactions and received 2,978 new service requests citywide, while closing 3,030 requests. Illegal/abandoned parking was the top service request type both citywide and in every individual ward.
APR-010.key_stats: Total constituent interactions: 2,819 (1,963 calls answered, 401 voicemails, 439 emails, 16 online chats); New service requests: 2,978; Closed service requests: 3,030; Top request citywide: Parking Illegally/Abandoned (951); Largest department by new requests: Reno Police & Parking Enforcement (1,376 new, 1,472 closed).

APR-015.title: Clean and Safe Team Homeless Actions and Outcomes - March 2026
APR-015.tldr: The Clean and Safe Team's March 2026 report summarizes interdepartmental efforts to address homelessness in Reno, including 41 outreach interactions, 4 housing arrangements, 96 arrests, and 22 encampment clean-ups. Service requests totaled 351, a 9% increase over February, driven largely by a 35% rise in Occupied Vehicle Nuisance reports. Outreach facilitated motel assistance placements, Safe Camp referrals, and coordination with regional partners. Mobile camera trailers continue to deter chronic encampment activity at key locations.
APR-015.key_stats: 41 outreach interactions; 4 housing arrangements; 1 shelter arrangement; 176 citations; 96 arrests; 3 large and 19 smaller encampment clean-ups; 164 yards of trash collected; 50 occupied vehicles posted; 15 occupied vehicles towed; 6 park exclusions; 33 Code Enforcement cases; 138 Allied Security site visits; 351 total Reno Direct service requests (9% increase over February); Occupied Vehicle Nuisance requests up 35% (148 to 200); Camping in City Parks down 47% (17 to 9)
APR-015.key_decisions: Mobile Camera Trailer Pilot Program remains ongoing at Spokane Street and Galletti Way; 1 individual facilitated into Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program; 1 individual transitioned from Motel Assistance Program into permanent housing; 2 individuals referred to and approved for Safe Camp; collaboration initiated with University of Nevada Reno Police Department regarding problem areas surrounding the university
APR-015.action_items: UNR StreetMed Team to follow up and treat wounds of Siegel Suites - Nevadan client enrolled in Reno Housing Authority Motel Assistance Program; continued monitoring of Our Place waitlist status for daughter and granddaughter; ongoing mobile camera trailer deployment at Spokane Street and Galletti Way locations

FEB-007.title: December 2025 City Walk Outcomes
FEB-007.tldr: This memo reports on City Walk operations conducted throughout December 2025, covering 16 walk dates across various Reno neighborhoods and the Downtown Business Improvement District. Participating departments included Clean and Safe, Code Enforcement, Reno Police Department, and Parks and Recreation. Outcomes included individual outreach contacts, nuisance vehicle notices, code enforcement cases, citations, and trash removal. The program coordinates proactively with Reno Direct service request data and the RPD DEEDS zones to connect residents with services and enforcement.
FEB-007.key_stats: 16 walk dates in December 2025; 1 individual accessed REMSA services; approximately 75+ individuals contacted across all walks (majority declined services); 12 nuisance vehicles issued 24-hour notice postings; 18 code enforcement cases created (broken windows, debris, graffiti, unsecured fencing, outdoor storage, trespassing, lighting, vegetation); 11 citations issued for sitting/lying/sleeping/camping in public places; 14 yards of trash removed; 1 NDOT request submitted for unsecured fencing
FEB-007.action_items: 1 request submitted to Nevada Department of Transportation (NDOT) for unsecured fencing (December 1 walk); ongoing coordination with Downtown Reno Partnership on Tuesday and Thursday BID walks; continued interdepartmental coordination with RPD DEEDS zones.
